We have experiemented with the YARD Sticks for a while (thanks again!) and came to the conclusion that a YARD Stick support does not comply with the URH philosophy. Why that?
- YARD Stick is designed to work with already known protocols (byte patterns), URH instead helps to find out the byte patterns and protocol fields
- YARD Stick does not output I/Q data, so we cannot handle it as a SDR (like all other devices we are supporting)
- RfCat (or Rflib) is used as interface to the YARD Stick. However, RfCat (hopefully not the hardware) does not run stable when receiving longer byte streams. There are USB timeouts that prevent from using the byte stream in a reliable way.

Never say never, maybe we support the YARD Stick in some way in the future. To everyone who is using the YARD Stick to pentest/reverse engineer protocols. Which kind of support would help you? A plugin showing the approximated YARD Stick (RfCat) parameters when sniffing with e.g. a RTLSDR? Please be precise and give some good arguments :)

You are right! Good catch. The concept of abbreviations came after the first definition file. Feel free to fix them in default_en.txt. The rule is quite simple, if the equal signs (`=`) defines positions
- the first is the canonical name of the unit.
- the third is the symbol/abbreviated name

So:
<Canonical name> = <definition> = <symbol/abbreviated name>

@sergiocorreia, the original concern is that once filtering is allowed, there's no upper limit on the size of CSV. And then I know there can be very big CSV in data science. In that case, it is well-known that Pandas does it much faster.

But then I discovered other limitations from Pandas' CSV parser that won't be ideal for pantable. And also taken into account performance is not too critical here, so we should only worry it when it becomes a real problem.

Also note that a more important criteria on performance in this case is actually memory use, not CPU time. I didn't profile it yet, but I guess Python's implementation will waster a lot of memory, because low memory usage is very hard to achieve.

But working on something related to pandoc (and LaTeX) means these are not very important, because pandoc (and LaTeX) themselves are already a bottlenect in some way. (pandoc's AST model, perhaps also because of its design, and use of Haskell, makes high memory use unavoidable.)

It's weird that the pure python implementation works OK. I benched the pure python implementation and the C extension module before and the results told me that the C version was faster. However, I didn't measure the CPU utilization rate.

I have to tackle with this problem: I have a SetOf() made of Attributes() derived from OctetString:
```
class AttributeValue(OctetString):
    # AttributeValue ::= OCTET STRING
    # encoding = 'utf-8'
    pass

class Vals(SetOf):
    # vals       SET OF value AttributeValue }
    componentType = AttributeValue()
```

usually attributes values are encoded in utf-8, so I used to define the (now commented) ``encoding = 'utf-8'`` in Attribute().

In some specific case the Attribute value encoding is different, but I know this only at runtime. For example when setting the user password in Active Directory encoding is ``utf-16-le``. So I removed the ``encoding='utf-8'`` because I get ``pyasn1.error.PyAsn1Error: Can't decode string 'b'"\x00R\x00c\x005\x006\x007\x008\x001\x002\x00\xe0\x00\xe8\x00\xec\x00\xf2\x00\xf9\x00"\x00'' with 'utf-8' codec at 'AttributeValue'` when trying to store the value as a string`. This makes sense because the value is encoded in ``utf-16-le``. Now the default ``iso-8859-1`` encoding applies but it returns a non-sense string.

How can I specify the encoding for the AttributeValue() in the Vals() setOf at runtime? I've found that setting encoding='utf-8' it's not enough, I must set _encoding too. The _encoding should be private to pyasn1 so I should not access it. There is a better way to do this? Alternatively I should return the byte stream instead of string representation in case the pyasn1 exception raises, but in this case the string should be not easily readable.

Original comment by: Alan Franzoni

If you do a release of secretstorage that relies on jeepney, specify `python_requires=">=3.5"` in your setup.py ([setuptools docs](https://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html#new-and-changed-setup-keywords)). When a user runs `pip install` on an older version of Python, it will respect that restriction, skipping over the incompatible release to automatically install an older version of the package.

We have done this for IPython 6.x releases, for instance. When someone does `pip install ipython` on Python 2.7, they still get the latest 5.x version.

Pip 9 was the first release that implemented support for this - that's already over a year old. But it's still worth having a separate check of `sys.version_info` in setup.py, to catch people installing with older versions of pip, or with other tools.

In my use-cases, I typically would cat and then merge, so the BedTool.cat() method does a post-merge unless you tell it not to; additional kwargs are sent to the merge command after cat-ing.  Also keep in mind that the latest BEDTools version requires that a file be sorted before merging, but the command in BedTool.cat takes care of that.

However, you're doing a slop call before merging.  So something like this should do the same as the command you showed:

```python
import pybedtools
results = pybedtools.BedTool('file1.bed')\
              .cat('file2.bed', post_merge=False)\
              .slop(genome='hg19', b=10)\
              .sort()\
              .merge()
```

I have a theory.

In your code, you're doing this:

```python
dir_path = os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(__file__))
sys.path.insert(0,dir_path + "colorama")
```

But this isn't correct! If this file is located in, say, `/path/to/code/main.py`, then `dir_path` will contain `/path/to/code`. When `colorama` is appended to that, then you end up with `/path/to/codecolorama`, which probably isn't what you intended and likely doesn't even exist. (The fix is to use `os.path.join(dir_path, 'colorama')`.)

Since `/path/to/codecolorama` doesn't exist, Python can't import colorama from there. In the end, it looks in the directory housing `main.py`.

Now's the golden part. Since there's a colorama folder in the directory with `main.py`, Python loads up that folder as a [namespace package](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0420/#examples). That's why `colorama.__path__` says `_NamespacePath`.

**TL;DR:** Don't try to combine paths using `+`. Use `os.path.join`.

In the specification I am writing against, a key with a value of 'null' is required to be omitted from the output. 

So if we have `{"foo": "bar", "data": "something"}` the output should be `{"foo": "bar", "data": "something"}`. But if we have `{"foo": "bar", "data": None}` the output should be `{"foo": "bar"}`. 

The behaviour in my use case is to omit the field entirely from the _output_ if the value is `null`. I would have thought `required=False` would trigger that behaviour, but I think there's some uncertainty about what component of the value is "required." Is it "required" on the input data? Or "required" to be in the output serialization?

Looking at this further, this specific example works for me (`get_by()` returns the one object, `.filter()` returns one object in a list). Did you maybe add the `unique=True` after initially saving the object? Either way, that doesn't mean that there doesn't exist a problem in general - in fact, using a string column to store what looks to be an integer value will result in additional Redis memory usage over using an `Integer()` column with `index=True` and `unique=True`. But at least for this very specific use-case, what you are doing *should* work.

As for being able to override the primary key, this is what I use for my own caching:
```
class MyModel(Model):
    id = PrimaryKey()

MyModel.id = Integer(required=True)
```

There are several caveats when using this method:
* Unique indexes are not supported, though they are sort-of implied by the fact that there is one place to store data with any individual id, but you have to use `.get(id)` and not `.get_by(id=id)`
* If you want to be able to perform numeric range queries, you will need to add `index=True` to both the `PrimaryKey()` definition and the `Integer()` replacement
* If you do `MyModel(id=5)` in one place and `MyModel(id=5)` later, any subsequent `.save()` may override data, and may partly or fully break other indexes - always use a decent locking mechanism to ensure that you're not breaking your indexes (I can explain more about this later, as this might be really important)

So... long story short; you can do this, but it's going to look different, and you need to be careful when updating/writing data to not break some of your indexes. Something like...
1. Acquire lock for object with primary key X
2. `a = RomModel.get(X) or RomModel(pk=X)`
3. Update/modify the object `a`
4. `a.save()`

`rom` doesn't currently have built-in locking functionality. You can use the `LuaLock()` implementation from the standard Python Redis client - it's generally good enough for this kind of thing.

Ok, what is interesting is that when you have it working with the kernel driver, you have pin 24 connected to reset, and pin 25 connected to the data/command line.

```
[   63.903337] fbtft_device: 'reset' = GPIO24
[   63.903345] fbtft_device: 'dc' = GPIO25
```

However, if you follow the instructions at https://luma-oled.readthedocs.io/en/latest/hardware.html#spi you will see that the _default_ pins for these two lines are the other way around.

Therefore I would suggest either:
* You connect the pins as suggested in the luma.oled documentation
* You maintain your existing pin connections and supply `--gpio-data-command=25` and `--gpio-reset=24` to override the defaults when running the demos.

Either should work.

That said, in that particular case, I would still do:

```
git checkout -b rtd_theme-squashed origin/rtd_theme 
git reset --soft origin/master
```

And then do one or two commits summarizing the changes and force push.

Then commit with a messages like these ones:

```
doc: Switch to rtd_theme

The existing theme was based of xxxx and had few problems like X, Y, Z. 
By switching to rtd_theme, ... 
```

```
doc: document command line interface introducing sphinx-argparse extension
```

Then `git blame` would behave nicely and allow to more clearly understand the change.

@brechtm , the changes which I feel warrant a minor version bump are mapping more fields from the BibTeX to the CSL data.  IMO adding fields that were not previously supported is a minor release, as that will cause information which was previously discarded to now appear in the output.  It isnt even strictly backwards compatible, as the same input will create a different output, but the input format is a fairly mature & stable and reasonably specified format so it is a bug that earlier versions discard parts of the input.  But people rely on such bugs after a while .. ;-)

```
[bumpversion]
current_version = 0.1.0

        \.(?P<minor>\d+)
        \.(?P<patch>\d+)
        ((?P<release>[a-z]+)(?P<num>\d+))?

        {major}.{minor}.{patch}

[bumpversion:part:release]
optional_value = placeholder
first_value = dev
values =
        dev
        placeholder
```

Intersection (give me all numbers whose codepoint is in this range) is definitely the correct behaviour. It's both necessary for the use case of "MySQL can't handle codepoints high enough to require 4 bytes in utf-8" and also seems like the one that makes most logical sense.

One complication is that this can result in an empty set of valid code points. If I ask for all surrogates in the range [0, 127] this has no valid characters. This should raise an InvalidArgument error (I think it's fine if any individual category misses the range, just not if all of them do).

Depending on the representation of our precomputed table and/or the size of the range this might be easy or hard to detect, which is why I thought it worth mentioning here. You definitely should not feel in any way obliged to do this unless you actually want to.

Yes, idstools can help you here. There are 2 programs, idstools-u2json and itstools-u2eve.  u2json is a pretty straightforward unified record to json converter.  So an event is a discrete json object, the following event is discrete, as is the extra data.  All the fields are preserved so you can correlate based on (event-id, event-second). So it might a little more raw than what you are looking for.

u2eve is an attempt to output, as close as possible, Suricata compatible EVE records. So you get your event along with one packet, then any tagged packets would follow as their own "packet" records.  They can be correlated with the original event as well.

I suggest you download and try them out on the command line to check the output of each.

Please see this example on how you might use it for continuous reading of your unified2 file:
http://idstools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tools/u2json.html#example-continuous-conversion-to-json

I'm not actively running Snort anymore, but I originally made these for easy sending to unified2 to Elastic Search, befure UnifiedBeats existed.

https://blog.jasonish.org/2014/04/16/snort-logstash-elastic-search-and-kibana/

> It looks like ansible_cmdb tries to parse every file recursively in the given inventory folder as a host file.

Are you sure? `group_vars` should be handled differently.

I'm not familiar with the following construction:

```
inventories/
  - company_name_dev/
```

I can't find anything about how ansible would handle such a construction. Do you happen to have a link to the documentation where this setup is described?

> group_vars/all.yml is parsed as a host file as well.

This issue has been fixed in a recent commit.

On a general note, I should probably say that I fear that variable parsing in ansible-cmdb will never be fully reliable. There are tons and tons of undocumented features and edge cases in Ansible itself and in the libraries it uses. The host / group vars inclusion in ansible-cmdb should be seen more as a bit of an extra rather than an authoritive source on which variables a given host has.

**Quick n dirty solution**: manually uninstall construct and re-install it at a specific version (`pip3 install construct==2.9.23`)

**Better solution**: explicitly require a specific version in `requirements.txt` to protect against unexpected API changes in dependencies.

Solved! It's an old OS X python issue that has either crept back in or was never fixed in Pdb. The fix is to set up to ignore SIGTTOU before importing readline. See http://bugs.python.org/issue14892 for details. 

I cured the problem by patching my  local copy of pdb.Pdb as shown below.

```
class Pdb(bdb.Bdb, cmd.Cmd):

    _previous_sigint_handler = None

    def __init__(self, completekey='tab', stdin=None, stdout=None, skip=None,
                 nosigint=False):
        bdb.Bdb.__init__(self, skip=skip)
        cmd.Cmd.__init__(self, completekey, stdin, stdout)
        if stdout:
            self.use_rawinput = 0
        self.prompt = '(Pdb) '
        self.aliases = {}
        self.displaying = {}
        self.mainpyfile = ''
        self._wait_for_mainpyfile = False
        self.tb_lineno = {}
        # Try to load readline if it exists
        try:
############ FIX OSX BUG ######################################
            import sys
            if sys.platform == 'darwin':
                import signal
                signal.signal(signal.SIGTTOU, signal.SIG_IGN)
############ END FIX ##########################################
            import readline
            # remove some common file name delimiters
            readline.set_completer_delims(' \t\n`@#$%^&*()=+[{]}\\|;:\'",<>?')
        except ImportError:
            pass               
``` 

Thanks much for your generous help and for hunter.

Cheers,
Mike

I guess the reason of '10 tasks takes more time for ThreadPool than 100 tasks' is four thread is too often try  get element from empty queue then raise exception. This action let Thread pool hard to find chance to put new job.

I do some test. I add sleep between each test to wait all thread done. And set different number of worker to test.

```python
import telebot
import threading
import time

def dummy_handler(): # takes roughly .05 seconds on my system.
    x = 0
    for _ in range(1000000):
       x += 1

pool = telebot.ThreadPool(1)

def test_thread_pool(x):
    for _ in range(x):
        pool.put(dummy_handler)


def test_thread(x):
    for _ in range(x):
        threading.Thread(target=dummy_handler).start()


def timef(func, *args, **kwargs):
    start = time.time()
    func(*args, **kwargs)
    return time.time() - start

for x in [1, 10, 100]:
    print "ThreadPool: {0} tasks, {1} seconds.".format(x, timef(test_thread_pool, x))
    #print "Single Threads: {0} tasks, {1} seconds".format(x, timef(test_thread, x))
    time.sleep(10)   # wait all thread done

pool.close()
```

I'm not necessarily against this syntax, though it seems a bit weird for a range to loop. Wikipedia doesn't describe this variant. Looking at the Go cronexpr library source here: https://github.com/gorhill/cronexpr/blob/master/cronexpr_parse.go#L381 (that should link to a "populateMany" function, which is what they use to handle ranges. Interpreting that code makes me think that they would produce no values, and not produce an error at all, though maybe they do earlier error checking that I'm not seeing.

I'm also not sure that this is necessarily unambiguous or not surprising behavior... Naively, a person may *expect* `*/30 23-05 * L *` to spill over and actually run on the 1st, but it wouldn't, because the 1st isn't listed in there. But `*/30 23-05 * 1,L *` *internally* would also run hours 0-5 on L, and 23 on 1. Or at least that is what would happen if I implemented this in the existing algorithm's structures (everything is checked against a *set* of items; if it matches, great, if not, try the next one - the go library does the same). In order to "properly" handle what may be a reasonable expectation of just running two separate cron schedules: `*/30 23 * L *` and `*/30 0-5 * 1 *`, to do what the syntax implied by the weird ordering would require a bunch of internal rewrites.

Can you use 2 cron schedules? Because looping ranges seems weird and not Pythonic (at least to me). Maybe I should just offer cron schedule math; where you can do (a + b) on two CronTab() things, and magic happens... or just pass `['*/30 23 * L *', '*/30 0-5 * 1 *']` and you get something magic on the other side.

I'd actually much rather do the CronTab() math or the passing the list of crontabs and getting magic, rather than supporting a looping range syntax, to be honest.

Escaping, newline, new paragraph
--------------------------------

When you use a ``{{ <var> }}``, you are modifying an **XML** word document, this means you cannot use all chars,
especially ``<``, ``>`` and ``&``. In order to use them, you must escape them. There are 3 ways :

   *  ``context = { 'var':R('my text') }`` and ``{{r <var> }}`` in the template (note the ``r``),
   *  ``context = { 'var':'my text'}`` and ``{{ <var>|e }}`` in your word template
   *  ``context = { 'var':escape('my text')}`` and ``{{ <var> }}`` in the template.

The RichText() or R() offers newline and new paragraph feature : just use ``\n`` or ``\a`` in the
text, they will be converted accordingly.

See tests/escape.py example for more informations.

To answer your question directly: no.

I don't think this project fits your use case very well. If you need to reference sections, I would recommend using a `ManyToManyField` to relate your model to the sections.

You should properly use relations in your database. This project is a hack on both relational databases and Django's ORM, and while that works just fine for a static list of non-relational choices, it doesn't work well if you remove either of those constraints.

If you really really want multivalued fields, I would recommend using `ArrayField` from `django.contrib.postgres.fields` (requires PostgreSQL).

I fully understand what pipenv brings to the table. Just to explain *why* I'm using it in docker:

- I need docker because the stack I'm running locally is complex. It's not just a single python app, it's a python app, database, mock servers, redis server, etc. All these need to be available, cross-platform, consistently between all devs on the team. Docker solves that.
- I need (want) pipenv because I need (want) to track my dependencies in a pipfile, rather than requirements.txt. That is to say, I'm moving the app to pipenv anyway. So now my choice is to either duplicate the dependencies, or use pipenv consistently in docker as well.

*With that said*, I'm not interested in solving my problem. I solved my problem by adding --skip-lock. It is seeing adoption, that doesn't mean that we can only write completely flat-toned, non-promotional, purely technical documents about it.  Sorry, I realize there is a lot of negativity and the desire to push back against this, but we are allowed to promote our own tools and use our own brands. We are also volunteers and interested in enjoying ourselves while we work.  Nothing about our participation in the PyPA mandates that we speak in pure monotone and technical specification.

In short, I'm not really interested in entertaining this kind of suggestion -- the honest approach is that we are allowed to market a product, and we are also allowed to build that product and simultaneously be part of the packaging authority.  We can be opinionated while doing all of these things and continue to use the branding of the person who designed the API behind it and whose idea it was to make this tool in the first place.

for the record I googled 'npm' and their landing page has a giant thing that says 'build amazing things' and no technical documentation until you scroll down a bunch.  So while I agree the project should speak for itself, I'm not really sure what that actually means or why it excludes promotional or opinionated material.  At the end of the day it describes the guiding philosophy (as compared with building things to solve technical challenges, or because some company paid you for it, etc).

> Packaging, as you mentioned, is more controversial for sure

I don't think anyone believes python packaging is in a good state. New users are expected to learn 3 different tools at a minimum to just install something, and if you don't first set up your path, locales, etc, you might wind up cross-pollinating your system with different versions of things and horribly breaking your installation before you've ever written a line of code.  This is not a good situation.

The brand was built when requests was built, and now it exists and it describes not just a negative ('this stuff is bad over here'), but instead a philosophy behind API design in general.  And no matter where this project lives, that philosophy is probably going to go with it, and so will the branding.

FYI, we've made some progress on this on the buildout side.

For now, this uses an extension:

  https://github.com/buildout/wheel/blob/master/src/buildout/wheel/__init__.py

That monkey patches setuptools to:

- treat ``.whl`` as a source extension and 

- augments ``interpret_distro_name`` to parse the project/version correctly.

When buildout sees a .whl distro, it converts it to an egg and the installs that.

The egg conversion currently uses humpty, which uses distlib, which seems heavy and buggy.  I'd like us to get rid of those dependencies, if practical.

At a minimum, we'll probably want to make a PR to optionally have setuptools treat wheels as source releases, to avoid monkey patching, although ideally we'd provide a PR to provide wheel support proper.

Just for the record, following a discussion as part of one of the JWST PSF photometry sprints, we had a re-thinking of the structure here. The idea we came up with will hopefully also resolve the problem of how to replace the existing `psf_photometry` class and a few other issues that have been rolling around in our collective heads.  This is the idea:
* Write a new class called something generic like `BasicPSFPhotometry`.  It would take all the same arguments as the current `DAOPhotPSFPhotometry` except `niters`, and it would mostly be a straight copy-and-paste of most of `DAOPhotPSFPhotometry` without the loop.  `do_photometry` for that would return just the table, *not* the subtracted image (although the users is of course free to do their own subtraction). `finder` can be None, which means just use the positions if they are given as an argument to `psf_photometry` (or if both are None raise an error)
* Replace the existing `DAOPhotPSFPhotometry` with a class called something like `IterativelySubtractedPSFPhotometry`.  This would look just like the current `DAOPhotPSFPhotometry`, but the internal implementation would just basically use `BasicPSFPhotometry` so it would end up a lot simpler than the current `DAOPhotPSFPhotometry`.  `do_photometry` would also just return the table, unlike the current one, which also returns the residual image, but it would do something like `self.residual_image = residual_image` at the very end so that the user can access it if they want it.  For this class `finder` *cannot* be None - attempting to do so should raise an error  
* Add a *subclass* of `IterativelySubtractedPSFPhotometry` called `DAOPhotPSFPhotometry`.  This would take all of the parameters that all of the *sub-objects* used in `DAOPhotPSFPhotometry` require, rather than explicitly setting e.g. the grouper, finder, etc.  Its implementation would be just an `__init__` which creates the relevant objects to reproduce the “daophot-style algorithm”

You can also override configuration directives using environment variables, leaving the .xml configuration schema alone.  The table below shows the relevant environment variables you can set, and the nupic configuration directives that they override.

|ENV var|nupic configuration directive|
|---|---|
|`NTA_CONF_PROP_nupic_cluster_database_passwd`|`nupic.cluster.database.passwd`|
|`NTA_CONF_PROP_nupic_cluster_database_host`|`nupic.cluster.database.host`|
|`NTA_CONF_PROP_nupic_cluster_database_user`|`nupic.cluster.database.user`|
|`NTA_CONF_PROP_nupic_cluster_database_port`|`nupic.cluster.database.port`|

For example:

```
export NTA_CONF_PROP_nupic_cluster_database_user=vshuk11
export NTA_CONF_PROP_nupic_cluster_database_passwd=mypasswd
```

@twiecki What I was going for initially is what was done in [this paper by Xiong and Ibbotson](http://corporate.morningstar.com/ib/documents/MethodologyDocuments/ResearchPapers/Momentum-Acceleration-and-Reversal.pdf) on page 9 in table 2 and 3.
![ibbotson table](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28760724/32754091-6999f1de-c89d-11e7-906f-16440e6cab13.PNG)

The above pretty much matches the grid/heatmap I posted in my first post (except I used the Fama French Factors). It did not use any form of regression. I simply took the mean of the double-sorted portfolio return bins. I then just plotted that table in a form that is often used to analyze interactions in [factorial experiments.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Factorial_experiment#Analysis_example) See below:
![factorial experiment](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28760724/32754212-e7b55856-c89d-11e7-97f2-744e5ccdb118.PNG) 

Regarding using a regression, I referenced this [article by Richard Williams at the University of Notre Dame](https://www3.nd.edu/~rwilliam/stats2/l55.pdf), which deals more specifically with interaction analysis of continuous variables. The process would be as follows:

1. Run a multivariate regression with an interaction term.
2. Choose a variable of interest to analyze the marginal effects.
3. Set the other variable to various "values of interest".
4. Calculate the slope given the regression coefficients and the value from step 3. 
5. Plot the different lines for various "values of interest".

Using the simulated data from earlier, this would come out as follows:

![formula](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28760724/32755204-2b3a1608-c8a2-11e7-929d-00676f510cac.PNG)

![ols results](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28760724/32755167-05c6c358-c8a2-11e7-8de8-8fbcfae3eda5.PNG)
![continuous factor interaction](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/28760724/32755949-ac4e22f4-c8a5-11e7-9bae-6703648115b3.png)


Hopefully this helps clarify. I appreciate the community's feedback.

A more general approach using a supplementary boolean argument stating if the user wants to restrict the computation to the stimulus interval with a default value to False (so that it does not break any existing code using eFel) might be desirable to the extent that this issue shows up in various features and duplicating all of them seem not desirable. 

I tend to consider the pre-stimulus period useful to let the transient dies out. If I'm exploring a space of possible modelling parameter values which contains some solutions that raise the equilibrium potential of my modelled cell, the transient from the initial membrane potential to the equilibrium can in some part of my parameter space create an initial spike which enters in the count. So, although my model stays completely flat line in response to the stimulus, the use of eFel lead me to believe that I am in a part of the parameter space in which my modelled cell is responsive, unless I eyeball every solution trace (which I might not do if I'm in a "pattern analysis and machine learning" kind of application). Importantly, it also affects many other features such as the between spike intervals (e.g., create a spurious 1s interval between between the spike due to transients and the beginning of the stimulus-related spiking from my stimulus in the [1, 2]s window). I had issues like this. I think @elisabettai also got spurious feature values related to similar issues. I guess may not be alone. Arguably, the user can add some code that ensure that the transients dies out before the actual simulation (or just cut the first 100 ms or so from the traces they pass to eFel), but this might not solve the issue when dealing with cells that can spontaneously fires some action potential at resting state (or at least, in the pre-stimulus window). As a general case, I would think this behaviour might not be intrinsically wrong. I could also imagine easily a case where I have spontaneous tonic firing, which frequency is modified by a stimulus step current and I want to extract this modified frequency. Aren't these use cases making sense? 

At the very lease, if you don't think it is desirable to add such a binary optional argument, I guess it would be a good idea to raise a warning when spikes from outside the stimulus period are detected and used in the computation of the features. The user can always disable such warning through standard python warning management code if they are annoyed by them in a case where spiking outside the stimulus interval is normal...

@albertc1 thanks for your feedback. Agree that using couple files makes it harder to grasp the whole test. We were thinking about such approach before implementing, but encountered couple of problems:

1. Consistency - for all supported databases we want to have similar fixtures api, but dbs have different syntax. And they are sometimes quite verbose, so data setup could take most place of the test code. 

2. Writing data to couple tables inside of one db - so simple param data=[...] will be not enough (in current implementation for example MysqlFixture), and needs more boilerplate. 

But anyway I'm good with implementing this, when we will have more or less consistent picture who to implement this. 

Quick solution for this I see is to make it possible to use strings together with file_path:
```
class MyTest(SparklyGlobalContextTest):
    data = """  
       CREATE TABLE ...
       INSERT INTO ...
    """
    fixtures = [MysqlFixture(host, port, data=data)]
```

More sophisticated solution, setup data with plain python structures:
```
class MyTest(SparklyGlobalContextTest):
    data = [{
      '_id': '1', 'attr_1': 'foo', 'attr_2': 'bar',
    },{...}]
    fixtures = [MysqlFixture(host, port, db, table, data=data),
                     ElasticFixture(host, port, index, es_type, data=data),]
```
So that each "fixture" instance corresponds to only one "table" in the database.

Thoughts?

Currently, when using the externally available protobuf library on App Engine, it is using the python-only version of the protobuf library (it's impossible to upload a C library to the python runtime). However, once we are able to get the external C library into the App Engine runtime it will start using the C version instead of the python version. The C version has faster encode/decode times however you do pay a penalty when accessing protocol buffer fields because it has to cross a process boundary. We saw this in NDB's Key implementation which tried to store everything internally in protobuf rather than storing the fields as native python types.

I went through and it looks like you only access the protobuf on serialization and deserialization, which is perfect, so I'm leaving this as closed.

```python
REQUIRED_PACKAGES = [
    'apache-beam[gcp]==' + PACKAGE_VERSION,
]
```

From the Apache Beam link above, the `gcp` extra has these requirements:

```python
GCP_REQUIREMENTS = [
    # oauth2client >=4 only works with google-apitools>=0.5.18.
    'google-apitools>=0.5.18,<=0.5.20',

]
```

I am a little concerned that users might not find it discoverable/intuitive.  We seem to have a growing number areas where the `aws-shell` diverges from `aws-cli`:

* Not requiring the `aws` command https://github.com/awslabs/aws-shell/issues/72
* Requiring the `!` prefix for shell commands https://github.com/awslabs/aws-shell/issues/19
* Requiring the `.` prefix for the special `cd` shell command (this issue)

I've added some more specifics on the concerns in the linked tickets.

I think the lowest friction approach for shell commands is to handle the 'magic' stuff in the background without users having to remember they need to use `!` or `.`.  From a technical perspective this could be more difficult, especially if you factor in some of the chaining/piping like James' example.

Just my 2 cents :)

    def do_open(self, http_class, req, **http_conn_args):
        """Return an addinfourl object for the request, using http_class.
    
            http_class must implement the HTTPConnection API from httplib.
            The addinfourl return value is a file-like object.  It also
            has methods and attributes including:
                - info(): return a mimetools.Message object for the headers
                - geturl(): return the original request URL
                - code: HTTP status code
            """
        host = req.get_host()
        if not host:
            raise URLError('no host given')
    
        # will parse host:port
        h = http_class(host, timeout=req.timeout, **http_conn_args)
        h.set_debuglevel(self._debuglevel)
    
        headers = dict(req.unredirected_hdrs)
        headers.update(dict((k, v) for k, v in req.headers.items()
                            if k not in headers))
    
        # We want to make an HTTP/1.1 request, but the addinfourl
        # class isn't prepared to deal with a persistent connection.
        # It will try to read all remaining data from the socket,
        # which will block while the server waits for the next request.
        # So make sure the connection gets closed after the (only)
        # request.
        headers["Connection"] = "close"
        headers = dict(
            (name.title(), val) for name, val in headers.items())
    
        if req._tunnel_host:
            tunnel_headers = {}
            proxy_auth_hdr = "Proxy-Authorization"
            if proxy_auth_hdr in headers:
                tunnel_headers[proxy_auth_hdr] = headers[proxy_auth_hdr]
                # Proxy-Authorization should not be sent to origin
                # server.
                del headers[proxy_auth_hdr]
            h.set_tunnel(req._tunnel_host, headers=tunnel_headers)
    
        try:
            h.request(req.get_method(), req.get_selector(), req.data, headers)
        except socket.error, err: # XXX what error?
            h.close()

```
file = drive.CreateFile({'title': 'my.pdf',
                         'parents': [{"id": '0B8K1Jq0f9odZNWlKUXMwZVJuZms'}]})
# Read file and set it as a content of this instance.

file.SetContentFile(file_path)

Implementation wise, here's my experience in building an efficient lazy dataset loader for another project.  This is for datasets larger then machine memory.  I don't know how easy/hard this would be to implement in the proposed APIs (as I'm having a hard time following the discussion without detailed knowledge of the current API).

On disk, the dataset is split into `N` random shards, where `N` might be 100-10,000.  Each shard can comfortably fit into memory.  To iterate through the data, the data loader picks a random shard, then treats it identically to a small dataset.  It randomly shuffles the shard, batches according to sequence length, indexes and places the resulting tensors onto a Queue.  This all happens in a separate process.  Then main modeling process then simply pulls batches from the Queue.  I found that using pytorch's monkey patched `multiprocessing` was much faster then the native Python one for tensors (e.g. `from torch.multiprocessing import Queue, Process`).  In this way, there is almost no overhead in the master process to read the data.

The metric paths on this collector are different depending on whether it's running on legacy mode or not.
Legacy mode is detected by the absence of a `vhosts` section in the configuration however, if in legacy mode, the collector introduces a `vhosts` section in the configuration programmatically and this causes the collector to be detected as non legacy on runs after the first, which in turn means that the metrics paths are different on every run after the first.

Removing the `vhosts` section after the run restores the legacy style configuration.

The definition used in [hctsa](https://github.com/benfulcher/hctsa) rescales relative to the second order moment, as is implemented in (the now long abandoned package) [TSTOOL](http://www.physik3.gwdg.de/tstool/).
But it also computes the raw version from Schreiber and Schmitz, Eq. (6), i.e., the numerator of the normalized statistic.
I'm not fully on top of the theory to provide much nuance as to which implementation makes more sense, but the nonlinear time-series analysis literature is a large one, and there have been lots of methods developed for detecting nonlinearity. hctsa also implements a range of higher order autocorrelations etc., and some surrogate data methods, all of which have been used in the literature to detect nonlinear time-series structure.

The dependency on the `ERC20BenchmarkConfig` in the setup and benchmark run methods stands out to me. Let's see if we can remove that dependency completely. Then each method gets access only to the pieces it needs, and returns the pieces needed by the next step. Also, let's see if we can find a way to remove state storage on the `config` instance between setup and benchmark run.

One option (which would require changes to the core benchmark runner) would be something like:

```py
def transfer_benchmark(w3, chain):
  # setup
  undeployed_contract = make_contract_for_deployment(w3)
  token_contract = erc20_deploy(w3, chain, undeployed_contract)
  txn_data = w3...buildTransaction().data
  yield

  # benchmark
  erc20_transfer(chain, txn_data, token_contract.address)
  yield

  # cleanup
  # (but no cleanup to do on this one, I think)
  yield

  # never hit this point
```

The test runner would roughly do:

```
def benchmark_run(benchmark: Callable):
  test_lifecycle = executor(w3, chain)
  next(test_lifecycle)  # setup

  start_clock ...
  next(test_lifecycle)  # run
  end_clock ...

  next(test_lifecycle)  # cleanup
```

> You are right in that Mayavi seems to require NumPy 10.9. 

That would certainly explain why the updates are occurring, then.

> But if that's the cause I am confused why $ conda update anaconda updated NumPy to 1.10 in the first place? 

Well, prior versions of `conda` would not consider all the packages in your environment when performing updates. This would frequently break environment consistency. For instance, doing a `conda update numpy` would update NumPy but it would not update the packages that *depend* on a particular version of NumPy.

Enter conda 4.x, which attempts to rectify this problem---but now it has to deal with environments that were broken by previous versions of conda. How should it attempt to gently guide these environments back to consistency? Our solution for now is to limit the environment updates only to the packages specified on the command line, and their dependencies and sub-dependencies. In most cases, this will result in environments slowly being corrected, at which point the new conda consistency enforcement kicks in full force.

I think you're seeing something along these lines. Certainly, `conda` is right to require numpy 1.9 to be installed. So basically, this isn't really a bug, but it is quite admitted

> And also that ...np110py27... --> ...np19py27... is labeled an "update".

Well yes, this makes sense, because the *package* is not being downgraded; just the particular *build* of the package is changing. NumPy itself may need to be downgraded, but the other packages just need to be changed to a NumPy 1.9 build.

@jettify well, yeah, indeed, `session_from_env` better than nothing but the problem here is it has to be used at all to achieve such a behavior. Here is a simple example that I faced last week: I have to use some apps to deal with resources that are available to me at this moment only via a proxy. Those apps are written in different languages such as Golang, C, Python etc. And none of their developers though about proxies when they were developing those apps so they didn't do anything special to support them. But thanks to standard libraries in those languages supporting this "de-facto standard" I was able to make those apps work just by executing while having `http_proxy` set. That's it, voila. This is a total win-win as on one hand developers didn't have to worry about proxies at all and I was able to use the tools without having to patch them.

Sure. You could use a `custom attribute` to satisfy your use case; it's just way more involved than what you need (and involves server feature support that is out of your control). You would need to do the following (at a minimum) to create and use a `custom attribute` for PyKMIP:

- Define an official name for it (e.g., 'x-Key-Application')
- Define what type of value you'd need to identify/store the attribute. In your case, it's just a string (an application name or ID). But you could easily add on additional pieces of information associated with the application (e.g., domain, permissions, account information), in which case you'd need to define a new struct to hold all of this information together. Regardless, a new object is needed.
- Define new tags (anything from 540000 - 54FFFF) to uniquely identify any new objects (strings, integers, etc) or structs you define for your attribute. Tags are needed at the byte level when encoding/decoding KMIP requests; without them, the client and server don't know what the values mean nor what they're for. (This has big implications for servers which I'll get to in a second.) (Note too that there are multiple tags for the same type of object. For example, both Issuer (tag 42003B) and Serial Number (tag 420087) are tags for string objects. The way both strings are encoded is the same, but the tag differentiates them. In your case, you'd need at least one new tag for the string or integer value used to differentiate applications.)
- Update the attribute factories to know how to build this new attribute.

This takes care of the client side of things, but you would still have a problem on the server side. KMIP clients and servers must agree on what tags they recognize, otherwise they cannot parse the request/response messages passed between them. If you add a custom attribute to the client, your server has to recognize what it is so that it can store it properly. It's not just a generic key/value store. If the server receives two strings in a request, it uses the tags to parse these values and tell them apart. If you want the server to be able to filter objects by your custom attribute when using the `locate` operation, it has to know how to do comparisons on it and those can change depending on the type. If the server does not recognize your custom attribute/tag, it would likely throw a parse error and reject your request as an invalid message.

The worst case scenario when adding custom attributes is a tag collision, where one brand of KMIP appliance reserves tag 540001 for one type while another brand reserves tag 540001 for a different type. This could create all sorts of problems when storing or retrieving your keys.

Did that help with your understanding? I'm happy to answer more questions if you have any.

Also, the more I think about it, I actually think `application specific information` might be most appropriate for you. It's literally just an `application namespace` string (e.g., 'Application') with a corresponding string value (e.g., 'A', 'B'). It's up to you which one you'd like to use. If `application specific information` seems good, you should check your appliance documentation to see if it supports `application specific information`, specifically if it puts any restraints on the `application namespace` value. Some appliances can be strict with attribute handling. If it looks like supporting your own arbitrary `application specific information` with your appliance could be challenging, just use an `object group` instead.

Regardless, I'll work on making sure all options are available through PyKMIP.

John McGrath <notifications@github.com> writes:

> So if I understand this correctly, `autodetected networks (-N)` and
> `autodetected hosts (-H)` will auto create a tunnel and forward the
> traffic but will not change the source IP address of my traffic. If I
> were to apppend `subnets` ex. `sshuttle subnets example.com:8000-9000
> -HNvr remoteUser@remoteServer` This would allow sshuttle to change the
> source IP address for only example.com and forward the rests of the
> traffic without changing the Source Ip address.

No, not sure you understand yet.

For ALL traffic forwarded through sshuttle, the TCP connection will
origingate from the sshuttle server. This means all TCP connections will
have the source IP address of the sshuttle server.

If the IP address is not being changed, this is a sure sign that
sshuttle is not forwarding the traffic. As sshuttle operates at the
application network layer it simply is not capable of forwarding to a
TCP connection without changing the IP address.

When using -N and -H, sshuttle will attempt to automatically detect what
traffic to forward. Based on magic I haven't really investigated. As a
result, it may not be forwarding the traffic you expect it to be
forwarding. Neither -N nor -H correspond to "forward all traffic".

In general it is much better to specificy the subnets you want
forwarded, rather then relying on sshuttle to do something
automatically, because this automatic use case probably won't suit your
needs.

> What should be returned for null/empty relationships if linkages are not supported by an API?

If an API supports compound documents, then it needs to support including linkage `data` for relationships to the included resources. Otherwise, there's no obligation to include relationship `data`. Regardless, if your API does include relationship `data`, then empty relationships should be represented as either `data: null` (to-one) or `data: []` (to-many).

Thanks for reporting this issue! Actually, I think Z3 is right in this case, but it's because of the uninterpreted function for the unspecified cases of (fp.min +-zero +-zero), so it's not quite trivial. My reasoning is as follows:

First, rewrite by naming the sub-expressions thusly:

    (set-logic QF_FP)

    (define-fun nz () (_ FloatingPoint 2 3) (_ -zero 2 3))
    (define-fun pz () (_ FloatingPoint 2 3) (_ +zero 2 3))

    (declare-fun x1 () (_ FloatingPoint 2 3))
    (declare-fun x2 () (_ FloatingPoint 2 3))
    (declare-fun x3 () (_ FloatingPoint 2 3))

    (assert (= x1 (fp.min nz pz)))
    (assert (= x2 (fp.neg x1)))
    (assert (= x3 (fp.min x2 pz)))

    (assert (= nz x3))

    (check-sat)

The `fp.max` around all of that is not necessary; the rewritten problem is unsatisfiable, and it becomes satisfiable if we change `(= nz x3)` into `(= pz x3)`. 

Now, all three variables can only be -zero or +zero (all other cases trivially unsatisfiable); so, let's case-split on x2: 

1) suppose x2 = +zero; then 
    x1 = fp.min -zero +zero
    (+zero = fp.neg x1) => x1 = -zero
    x3 = fp.min +zero +zero = +zero
    => x3 == -zero is false (regardless of x1).

2) suppose x2 = -zero; then
  x1 = fp.min -zero +zero (looks like "your choice of -zero or +zero")
  (x2 = fp.neg x1) => x1 = +zero
  x3 = fp.min -zero +zero (again "your choice of -zero or +zero", but, critically, you have to choose the same result as before for (fp.min -zero +zero)):

  A) suppose (fp.min -zero +zero) = +zero
    x1 = +zero
    x3 = +zero
    => x3 != -zero, contradiction with (= -zero x3).

  B) suppose (fp.min -zero +zero) = -zero
    x1 = -zero
    x3 = -zero
    x2 = (fp.neg x1) => x2 = +zero, contradiction with assumption 2).

Since fp.min has the same inputs, it must also have the same output, but that is impossible here. So, I think that's correct. Did I miss some crucial detail?

from django.conf import settings

> so, what is your suggestion about updating pca code in pytraj?

Two things:
1) If the user provides their own reference structure, do not create an average structure (there is no point). Also, not fitting at all should be considered a legitimate option - you may want to include global rotational and translational motion in your eigenvectors.
2) You should be able to supply separate masks for fitting and creating the covariance matrix. It is common enough for example to only perform rms-fitting on heavy atoms while still wanting all atoms in eigenvectors.

The coefficients are not built in (only up to 'db20' iirc), but you can create your own wavelet using `pywt.Wavelet(name, filter_bank=[dec_lo, dec_hi, rec_lo, rec_hi])`, where the dec/rec are the deconstruction and reconstruction coefficients, and lo/hi are the approximation and detail coefficients respectively.

I'm not aware of any plans to extend the builtin filters all the way up to db40, but it might be fun to implement the construction function for daubechies wavelets to allow arbitrary ones. Looks straightforward at first glance, I'll have a look in the coming week or so.

Hi all,

Ok I think I misunderstood. Yes GRDF/AFS are site-based features, but they can also be used to create Structure-based features. So they should be implemented as site-based features as the core, and there should be a separate structure-based feature that take the appropriate average/sum/concatenation of the site-based features.

As for feeding *everything* through SiteStatsFingerprint, I'd say:
- perhaps the concatenation ones (vector -> matrix) should be a different featurizer than the one that compute statistics. e.g. SiteMatrixFingerprint
- even if everything can technically be accomplished through SiteStatsFingerprint or SiteMatrixFingerprint, I think there is still value to having explicit classes for various features. For example, CoulombMatrix can continue to be a separate class with its own citations, etc., but maybe it will call SiteMatrixfingerprint internally for its operation (so the actual code will be very short). I think it will be too confusing to have SiteStatsFingerprint or SiteMatrixFingerprint be a Swiss Army knife that the user is expected to master directly in order to obtain the various types of fingerprints. And the presets can also get very unwieldy.
- For the structure featurizers returning the same number of features, certainly we try to follow those guidelines but unfortunately not all published methods do. What I thought we could do is add a ``TensorStats`` class similar to ``PropertyStats`` and rename ``PropertyStats`` to ``VectorStats``. Thus, ``VectorStats`` does things like average, stdev, etc. ``TensorStats`` would do things like compute eigenvalues and perhaps take the top N eigenvalues, or pad eigenvalues with zeroes to reach a certain number of elements, or compute a determinant, or return if it is symmetric (T/F), or do anything else that would transform a tensor quantity into either a vector or a scalar. Then the ``SiteMatrixFingerprint`` could take in certain ``TensorStats`` to compute to try to transform these matrixes into something more suitable for data mining.

Btw, we should probably decide on a nomenclature of ``Matrix`` or ``Tensor`` and stick to it. e.g., either ``SiteMatrixFingerprint`` and ``MatrixStats`` or ``SiteTensorFingerprint`` and ``TensorStats``. And perhaps we want to rename ``SiteStatsFingerprint`` to ``SiteVectorFingerprint``.

def _create_statement_edge(statement):
    edge_type = relay.Edge.for_node(StatementEntry)
    return edge_type(node=statement, cursor=base64.b64encode(str(statement.id)))

def resolve_bla(...):
    pagination_param = base64.b64decode(args['after']) if args.get('after') else None
    
    statements, has_next_page  = call_to_database_service(pagination_param)

    edges = [_create_statement_edge(statement) for statement in statements]
    connection_type = relay.Connection.for_node(StatementEntry)

    return connection_type(edges=edges, page_info=relay.PageInfo(has_next_page=has_next_page))

The [relay spec](https://facebook.github.io/relay/graphql/objectidentification.htm#sec-Node-root-field) explicitly states that a root node field must accept only a single argument of `id`.

You can have other [root fields](https://facebook.github.io/relay/graphql/objectidentification.htm#sec-Fields) which load data differently, but they have to fit with certain rules.

Of course if you are not building a GraphQL API for Relay, or your node is not at the root, then you can in theory do as you please.

Looking at the message you posted, "Unable to connect to server - failed to connect to the server, it usually happens due to firewall restrictions". If an external server is unable to connect to your server, there can be one of few problems.

- The apache server is not running.
- As mentioned, the firewall is blocking incoming connections. Could be a network firewall that sits before your server.
- Without more information, maybe a NAT device between your server and the internet? In which case, ports 80 and 443 need to be forwarded to your system.

Assume one has the following HTTPS redirection rule:
```
RewriteRule ^ https://%{SERVER_NAME}%{REQUEST_URI} [END,QSA,R=permanent] 
```
And lets say he updates his cert and hits the Secure option. Then the redirection rule would change to:

```
RewriteRule ^ https://%{SERVER_NAME}%{REQUEST_URI} [END,NE,R=permanent] 
```

The only problem is that if there are two (or more) names per VirtualHost (e.g. `example.com` and `www.example.com`, `Certbot` will output:
```
Enhancement redirect was already set.
Enhancement redirect was already set.
```
Which is its exact behavior that occurs without the PR (that is, Choosing "Secure" on a VirtualHost that `Certbot` already enabled https redirection and has two names). I believe this "bug" (?) should be addressed in a different PR.

(by stephen-tiedemann)
The MIUX parameter announced during link setup (LLCP Link MIU) does not govern the MIUX parameter for data-link-connections, these are both separate topics. The announced MIUX for the LLCP Link dictates the maximum number of octets that may be used for logical-data-links and other connection-less PDUs like AGF, CONNECT, SNL. The announced MIUX for a data-link-connection dictates the max number of octets in an LLC I-PDU, separately from the Link MIU. The default value, if not transmitted, for either MIU is 128.

`sos status ID ID ID` applies to all running tasks. What I meant was that previously, assuming `-J4`, we

1. submit IDs as four `sos execute ID`.
2. `sos status ID ID ID ID`
3. submit another four.

Right now, we

1. submit all IDs as four `sos execute ID ID ID ID ID ...` each with `700/4` tasks.
2. `sos status all IDs`
3. No need to submit more IDs. In practice, not all tasks were ready at step 1 so there can be one or more batches, but that is about it.

import tensorflow
from tensorflow.examples.tutorials.mnist import input_data
import time
import ray
import model

ray.init()
net = model.SimpleCNN()
all_keys, all_values = net.get_weights()

@ray.remote
class ParameterServer(object):
    def __init__(self, keys, values):
        # These values will be mutated, so we must create a copy that is not
        # backed by the object store.
        values = [value.copy() for value in values]
        self.weights = dict(zip(keys, values))

    def push(self, keys, values):
        for key, value in zip(keys, values):
            self.weights[key] += value

    def pull(self, keys):
        return [self.weights[key] for key in keys]

ps = ParameterServer.remote(all_keys, all_values)

@ray.remote
def worker_task(ps, batch_size=50):
    # Download MNIST.
    mnist = input_data.read_data_sets("MNIST_data", one_hot=True)

    # Initialize the model.
    net = model.SimpleCNN()
    keys = net.get_weights()[0]

    while True:

        weights = ray.get(ps.pull.remote(keys))
        net.set_weights(keys, weights)

        # Compute an update and push it to the parameter server.
        xs, ys = mnist.train.next_batch(batch_size)
        gradients = net.compute_update(xs, ys)
        ps.push.remote(keys, gradients)

worker_tasks = [worker_task.remote(ps) for _ in range(20)]

We explicitly disallow exposing such topological details and using them for placement. This leads to problems. Typically, there's an underlying reason why a user wants to place a task on a specific physical machine, e.g., data locality, task affinity, or some special resources the user knows are on that machine. In most cases you can use custom resource labels to achieve this. Here's a good starting point for using custom resources:
https://ray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/resources.html#custom-resources

> having pull request and issue data in two different indexes is ok for you, or do you see some problems to calculate the metrics in this way?

Depends. @jgbarah suggested that all the data related to PRs should stay in the PRs index. If we go with the first approach, then some of the PR related metrics will be calculated from the issues+pr index.

For example, for PRs only, the Metric [Maintainer Response to Merge Request Duration
](https://github.com/chaoss/wg-gmd/blob/master/activity-metrics/maintainer-response-to-merge-request-duration.md) will have to be calculated by taking into account both the indices(pull_requests only and issues+prs) and then taking the minimum of the first_response/attention_duration from both of the indices. It is not tough to do, so maybe we don't really need to fetch the comments data for pull_requests separately. 

It's just that I agree with Jesus that the data for issues and prs should be kept in separate indices. 
Jesus also suggested that we can probably combine both the indices using Elasticsearch Aliases.
